Trade-off: the plan is advisory, not an editable artifact you 'run your day from' across reloads.

Files: new src/lib/freeSlots.ts; edit TodayWidget.tsx (button + AutoPlanSection + reuse priorities()); add `effortMin?` to TopicItem in types.ts; optional tiny effort chip in DeadlineQuickAdd. No Dashboard wiring, no WidgetCtx change.

variants.name: Timed Blocks of Record (persist real PlannerItems, revive the dormant grid)
variants.approach:
The most ambitious 'a plan you run your day from' spine: the auto-planner WRITES real PlannerItem timed blocks (types.ts:216 already carries date+startMin+duration+done+topicId) into a newly-persisted store, making the plan a durable, editable artifact shared between the Briefing and the already-built-but-unmounted PlannerView day-grid.

Thread the write path the prompt calls for: add `plannerItems: PlannerItem[]` + `onPlannerItemsChange` to WidgetCtx (context.ts) and back it with a persisted state hook in Dashboard.tsx next to setDailyPlan (~line 1660), exactly mirroring how dailyPlan is persisted. Mount the dormant PlannerView (sections/PlannerView.tsx, currently imported by nobody) on the existing 'planner' SectionId so the auto-generated blocks are immediately visible AND drag/resize/done-toggle editable using code that already exists. This converts dead code into the editing surface for free.

Shared free-slot engine: same src/lib/freeSlots.ts freeGaps() as Variant A (the one-engine contract), but the packer emits `PlannerItem[]` (id:Date.now()+i, date:todayMidnight, startMin from the gap cursor, duration:effortMin, color from topic, topicId, done:false). On 'Auto-plan' click: compute, then onPlannerItemsChange([...existing-for-other-days, ...newBlocks]) after clearing today's auto-blocks (tag them with an `auto:true` flag added to PlannerItem so re-plan only nukes machine-made ones and preserves hand-placed blocks). Mark completion bi-directionally: toggling a PlannerItem done can advance the linked topic item's stage via onAdvanceStage(topicId,...), so 'running your day' updates the real task graph, not a throwaway list.

Brief rendering: TodayWidget reads plannerItems for today and renders the same 'Free 10-12 -> Stats essay' summary as Variant A, but now it is a live mirror of persisted blocks, with a 'Open planner' link into the full grid. The honest '2 bumped to tomorrow' line offers a one-tap 'push bumped to tomorrow' that writes those as tomorrow blocks.

This is the boldest, most defensible against Sunsama/Akiflow because it produces a genuine timeline you edit and re-run, and it pays down debt by activating PlannerView and PlannerItem. Trade-off: biggest surface area (new WidgetCtx field + Dashboard persisted state + mounting a view + dedup/auto-tag logic), so highest TS/regression cost of the three, though still 100% frontend and schema-free (PlannerItem already exists).

Files: new src/lib/freeSlots.ts; types.ts (effortMin? on TopicItem, auto? on PlannerItem); context.ts (plannerItems+onPlannerItemsChange); Dashboard.tsx (persist plannerItems, pass to Briefing ctx, mount PlannerView on 'planner'); TodayWidget.tsx (button + read/render blocks); reuse PlannerView.tsx as-is.

variants.name: Timeboxed dailyPlan (one map, add an order+time overlay, no new store)
variants.approach:
A middle path that resolves the prompt's explicit tension ('dailyPlan maps dateKey->ids with NO time-of-day, so this round must thread a timed-block write path') by UPGRADING the existing dailyPlan structure in place rather than adding a parallel PlannerItem store: keep one source of truth, give it time-of-day.

Data change (backward-compatible, no schema/Supabase touch since DailyPlan is a client TS type): widen DailyPlan from `Record<string,string[]>` to `Record<string, (string | {id:string; startMin:number; duration:number})[]>` OR, cleaner, a sibling map `dailyPlanTimes: Record<dateKey, Record<itemId,{startMin,duration}>>` persisted next to dailyPlan in Dashboard. A migration-safe reader treats any id lacking a time entry as 'unscheduled' (today's existing behaviour), so old plans keep working and DailyPlannerView's assign/unassign keep operating on the id list untouched. Only the new auto-planner writes the time overlay.

Shared free-slot engine: identical src/lib/freeSlots.ts freeGaps() (the one-engine contract powering P1+P2). 'Auto-plan' takes priorities() unscheduled tasks plus any already-assigned-but-untimed dailyPlan[today] ids, packs them into gaps using effortMin (new TopicItem field), and writes ONLY the time overlay via onDailyPlanChange/a new onDailyPlanTimesChange — no new top-level WidgetCtx array, just one companion map threaded the same way dailyPlan already is (Dashboard ~1660, setDailyPlan pattern).

Rendering: TodayWidget's existing TasksSection (which already merges planned + due rows) gains a time prefix per row ('10:00 Stats essay') sorted by startMin, and the brief headline shows the 'Free 10-12 -> ...' summary + 'full, 2 bumped' line exactly as specified. Because it is the same dailyPlan the DailyPlannerView edits, dragging a task to another day in the planner naturally drops its time (reader falls back to untimed), so the two surfaces stay coherent without revival of PlannerView.

Why distinct: Variant A persists nothing, Variant B builds a second timed store + revives a grid; this variant has exactly one plan structure (the one users already curate) that simply learns about time, minimizing duplicate-source-of-truth bugs while still being durable across reloads. Trade-off: touches the DailyPlan type and its readers (TodayWidget line ~351, DailyPlannerView itemsForDay), and the overlay-map approach is slightly awkward vs a first-class block, but it sidesteps mounting/maintaining the heavyweight PlannerView grid.

Files: new src/lib/freeSlots.ts; types.ts (effortMin? on TopicItem; optional widen/companion DailyPlanTimes type); context.ts + Dashboard.tsx (persist + pass companion times map); TodayWidget.tsx (button + timed TasksSection); light read-only awareness in DailyPlannerView.

experiments.winner: Inline Brief Schedule (derive-on-the-fly, zero new persistence)

votes.preferred: Inline Brief Schedule (derive-on-the-fly, zero new persistence)
votes.why:
I'm Maya, first-year, and what I actually want at 8am is to press one button and see "Free 10-12 -> Stats essay, 14-15 -> reading" right there on my morning brief. I do NOT want to "run my day from a timeline" - I want to be told what to do next and then go to lectures and forget the app exists. Variant A gives me exactly the screenshot moment with the least that can go wrong.

The self-healing is the real reason I'd pick it. My timetable shifts constantly (rooms move, a seminar gets cancelled, I tick a task done on my phone). Variant A recomputes from live signals every render, which is already how the rest of the Today widget behaves (priorities() is recomputed every render - I can see that in TodayWidget.tsx). So my plan is never stale and never lying to me. Variants B and C persist time-of-day blocks, which means the moment my 2pm class moves, my "plan of record" is wrong until I go re-edit it - that's the busywork that makes me abandon planner apps in the first place. An advisory plan that's always right beats an editable plan that's usually wrong.

As Maya I also won't ever open a separate full-screen day-grid. Variant B's whole payoff is reviving PlannerView.tsx and the PlannerItem timeline - but I confirmed PlannerView is imported by nobody and the 'planner' SectionId has no mounted component (Dashboard.tsx only mounts DailyPlannerView). So B is paying its largest cost (new WidgetCtx field, new persisted store in Dashboard, mounting dead code, auto:true dedup tagging, bi-directional done-sync) to build an editing surface I, the target user, would basically never touch. That's a tool-builder's win, not a student's.

Variant C is the tempting middle, but as a user I get almost the identical visible result as A (a timed line in TasksSection, which already merges planned+due rows at line 379) while it quietly widens the DailyPlan type and adds a companion times map that every reader has to stay migration-safe about. The durability it buys me is marginal because I'd just re-press Auto-plan tomorrow anyway - I don't reload mid-day and expect yesterday's exact timeboxes back.

So: A delivers the signature DOING moment I'd genuinely use, ships fastest (one new pure file src/lib/freeSlots.ts, button + section in TodayWidget.tsx, one optional effortMin? field on TopicItem), keeps TypeScript green with the smallest surface, and is the safest against the prompt's own 'dailyPlan has no time-of-day' trap because it simply never persists time-of-day. The one trade-off (the plan isn't an editable artifact) is a feature for me, not a loss - I want guidance, not another grid to maintain. The shared freeSlots.ts engine is built either way and still feeds the P2 effort-warning banner and start-now nudge, so picking A costs me nothing on the roadmap.

votes.preferred: Inline Brief Schedule (derive-on-the-fly, zero new persistence)
votes.why:
As Tom, a freelancer, the thing I actually want from "Auto-Plan My Day" on the Briefing is the 90-second morning answer: "here's where your work fits today." I want to glance, trust it, and start. I do NOT want a second timeline to maintain. My day moves constantly (a client call slips, a job gets pulled forward), and the killer feature of Variant A is that it self-heals: it's recomputed each render from live signals, so when a meeting moves or I tick a task done, the plan just re-derives. That is exactly the freelancer reality. A persisted plan (Variant B/C) becomes stale the moment my real calendar diverges, and then I'm babysitting blocks instead of working, which is the Sunsama/Akiflow chore I'm trying to escape.

I verified the variants against the real code, and it reinforces the pick. Variant A reuses machinery that already exists and is proven: getStartMin/getEndMin minute math in TodayWidget.tsx (lines 44-54), the priorities() engine (line 70, overdue->today->week), and the existing TasksSection that already merges planned+due rows. The only data change is one optional effortMin? on TopicItem. That means it ships fast, stays TypeScript-green, and carries near-zero regression risk, which matters in an aggressive dogfood loop where I want the screenshot moment THIS round, not a refactor.

I considered Variant B (persist real PlannerItems, mount the dormant PlannerView). It is genuinely the most "defensible product" on paper, and the code backs its claims: PlannerItem already exists fully-formed in types.ts (lines 216-228) with date+startMin+duration+done+topicId, and PlannerView.tsx is imported by nobody so it really is free editing-surface code. But as the actual user I'd rather not get an editable artifact yet; it adds the biggest surface area (new WidgetCtx field + Dashboard persisted state + mounting a view + auto-tag dedup), and the bi-directional done/stage sync is exactly where subtle bugs live. Right now I trust a fresh advisory line more than a durable timeline I have to keep honest. Note also that the 'planner' SectionId currently has no view mounted, so B is mounting a never-shipped grid, more unknowns.

Variant C (timebox the existing dailyPlan via a companion times map) is the tempting "one source of truth" compromise, and I respect that it avoids a duplicate store. But it touches the DailyPlan type and every reader, and the overlay map (id->{startMin,duration}) is the awkward in-between: it has the persistence/staleness downside of B without giving me the real first-class editable block, plus the silent-time-drop-on-drag behavior is the kind of "why did my time disappear?" surprise that erodes trust.

So: start with Variant A. It's the honest, self-healing, demo-able spine that nails my morning, and critically it builds the shared src/lib/freeSlots.ts engine that B or C could later sit on top of. If the advisory plan proves I actually want to "run my day from it" across reloads, graduate to B then. Ship the cheap, trustworthy win first.

votes.preferred: Inline Brief Schedule (derive-on-the-fly, zero new persistence)
votes.why:
As Sam, a power user who lives in the Briefing and re-plans constantly when classes move, I'd actually prefer Variant A. I verified the codebase to ground this, and the deciding factor is that A's promise is the only one that's fully honest about the data reality.

What I confirmed in the repo:
- src/lib/types.ts: TopicItem (line 21) has NO effortMin yet (all three add it the same way), DailyPlan (line 234) really is Record<string,string[]> with no time-of-day, and PlannerItem (line 216) really does already carry date+startMin+duration+done+topicId.
- TodayWidget.tsx already has the exact engine pieces A reuses: getStartMin (44), getEndMin (50), priorities() (70), and it already reads dailyPlan[todayKey] (351, 508). So A's "reuse the minute math you already have" is true, not aspirational. The free-slot engine is a small, pure, testable addition with zero wiring risk.
- The critical fact that sinks Variant B for me: PlannerView.tsx (sections/PlannerView.tsx:66) is imported by NOBODY in Dashboard.tsx, and there is NO `activeSection === 'planner'` render branch at all (only 'dailyPlanner' -> DailyPlannerView at line 1763). So B isn't "reviving dormant code" cheaply; it's standing up an unmounted, untested grid AND adding a brand-new persisted WidgetCtx array AND bi-directional done<->stage sync. That's the largest surface area and the highest regression risk, and as the user I don't get proportional value on day one: I want the headline "Free 10-12 -> Stats essay" line, which all three give me.

Why A over C specifically: C's instinct (one source of truth) is the most architecturally appealing, and I respect that it avoids a duplicate store. But it pays for durability by widening the DailyPlan type or bolting on a companion dailyPlanTimes map, then editing every reader (TodayWidget ~351, DailyPlannerView itemsForDay). The trade-off C itself admits, dragging a task to another day silently drops its time, is exactly the kind of quiet incoherence that erodes my trust in an auto-planner. And the moment my auto-plan persists, I expect to edit it; C gives me persisted times with no real editing surface for them (it explicitly avoids reviving the grid), so I'd have a stored schedule I can't directly manipulate, which is the worst of both worlds.

A's "advisory, recomputed every render" model is the right v1 for the signature DOING move precisely because of how I behave: my calendar shifts, tasks complete, and I re-plan multiple times a day. A self-heals automatically because it derives from live signals every render, with zero migration and zero "stale persisted block" bugs. The honest "2 bumped to tomorrow" footer and the per-row deep-link via setActiveSection (the same pattern PrioritiesSection already uses) give me the screenshot-worthy moment and a real path into the task. It ships this round with confidence, keeps TypeScript green, touches no auth/payments/schema, and crucially earns the right to graduate to a persisted artifact later, once the free-slot engine and effortMin have proven themselves. Build the one-engine contract (freeSlots.ts) now as A, and B/C become a natural follow-up rather than a risky big-bang.

experiments.priority: Action layer: one-keystroke snooze on every row + an end-of-day close-out with clear-streak

variants.name: Inline Snooze Stepper + Auto-Celebrating Today Card
variants.approach: Tightest, lowest-risk read: bolt the whole loop into TodayWidget with zero new surfaces. SNOOZE: every PrioritiesSection row (mirrored on DeadlinesView rows) gets a hover/focus 'zzz' button opening a 3-chip popover Tomorrow / Weekend (next Sat) / Next week (+7d). Picking one recomputes item.deadline to that day's local-midnight (keeping dueMin), rebuilds the Topic, and calls ctx.onTopicChange(next); priorities() recomputes from topics so the row re-buckets instantly. With a row focused, keys 1/2/3 fire the presets directly = the literal one-keystroke promise. Imported 'calendar' rows (negative id, not topic-owned) show no snooze button - a clean honest degrade respecting the mutation constraint. PAYOFF: TodayWidget already computes prios/overdueCount/dueTodayCount. When actionable today+overdue priorities transition to zero AND there was >=1 earlier, swap the empty section for a ClearCard: checkmark, 'Today's done - N cleared', the SummaryLine promise finally paid, plus flame + streak. Streak reads a new top-level synced key clearLog (Record<dayKey,true>) written the moment zero is hit (mirroring HabitsWidget.toggle setting entries[todayKey]=true), with the number from the same backward-walk as currentStreak(). A secondary 'Roll N unfinished to tomorrow' appears only if overdue/today items remain, re-dating each owned topic item via batched onTopicChange. One file does almost everything; snooze on DeadlinesView reuses the same preset helper. Best for shipping fast with the morning brief as single source of truth.

variants.name: Shared useSnooze hook + dedicated EndOfDay close-out ritual
variants.approach: Treats the two halves as reusable infrastructure so the loop is consistent everywhere and the reward gets ceremony. SNOOZE as a shared primitive: extract useSnooze(ctx) (new file src/components/widgets/useSnooze.ts) exposing snoozeItem(section,itemId,preset) plus preset math (tomorrow/weekend/next-week/this-evening); it resolves the topic by section, rewrites deadline/dueMin, and calls onTopicChange - the single audited mutation path. A matching <SnoozeMenu> renders consistent chips + 1/2/3 hotkeys, dropped into PrioritiesSection rows, DeadlinesView rows, and (free bonus) UpcomingDeadlinesWidget, so 'push to tomorrow' is identical app-wide. Snooze only re-dates, so P2's 'bumped to tomorrow' chip can detect item.deadline===tomorrow and label itself, making snooze and the plan visibly one system. PAYOFF as a first-class ritual: instead of an inline card, add a lightweight EndOfDay close-out - a dismissible banner/sheet atop Home that appears once today's actionable priorities hit zero, plus an always-present 'Close out the day' button in TodayWidget's footer for manual firing. It shows 'N cleared today', the clear-streak (new synced clearLog key, same save()/reload()+backward-walk as habits), a 7-dot week heatmap of recent clear days (directly answering 'Habits gets a heatmap, this gets nothing'), and a primary 'Roll M unfinished -> tomorrow' that re-dates owned items AND seeds dailyPlan[tomorrowKey] with their ids via onDailyPlanChange so tomorrow opens pre-planned. Manual close-out lets the reward fire even on a not-quite-empty day, matching real student behaviour. Best for coherent reused snooze across surfaces plus an event-like reward, at the cost of one component + hook.

variants.name: Triage Mode: focused clear-one-by-one inbox over the priorities
variants.approach: Boldest, most 'inbox you clear' interpretation - turn morning triage into a dedicated single-card flow rather than decorating the list. A 'Clear today' entry point in TodayWidget opens a focused mode (overlay or dedicated section) showing ONE priority at a time, large and centered, with four always-visible actions + hotkeys: Done (onAdvanceStage to the done stage / complete), Snooze-> (tomorrow/weekend/next-week presets rewriting deadline via onTopicChange), Edit deadline, Skip. Arrow/J-K and 1-4 drive it entirely from the keyboard so a user blasts the list in seconds - the snooze half becomes the spine of the interaction, not a per-row accessory. A progress rail ('3 of 7') sits on top. When the last card clears, the mode resolves into the celebration state: 'Today's done - N cleared', the persisted clear-streak (new synced clearLog key via the same save()/reload()+backward-walk pattern as habit streaks), and a single 'Roll unfinished -> tomorrow' for anything Skipped, re-dating those owned topic items to tomorrow's midnight. Imported calendar rows appear but only offer Done/Skip (no snooze), honouring the no-mutation constraint. A lightweight per-row inline snooze can remain as a fallback for list users, but the headline is the focused triage. This reframes the static guilt list as an active 'process my morning' ritual with a finish line and reward - highest engagement payoff and most differentiated, at the cost of the largest build (new focused-mode component + keyboard state machine) while still touching only frontend state via existing audited paths.

experiments.winner: Shared useSnooze hook + dedicated EndOfDay close-out ritual

votes.preferred: Shared useSnooze hook + dedicated EndOfDay close-out ritual
votes.why:
As Maya (first-year, living out of the morning brief), this is the one I'd actually use day to day. My real problem isn't "I need a fancy clearing flow," it's that the same task shows up on me in three places (Today's Priorities, the deadlines list, Upcoming) and I want to push one thing to tomorrow without thinking. Variant 2 makes snooze identical everywhere because it's one shared primitive (useSnooze + SnoozeMenu dropped into PrioritiesSection, DeadlinesView, AND UpcomingDeadlinesWidget). The codebase backs this: priorities() recomputes straight from topics and onTopicChange(next) is the single mutation path, so a snooze re-dates the item and the row instantly re-buckets in every surface at once. Variant 1 only wires snooze into TodayWidget rows, which means when I'm staring at my deadlines page (where I actually panic about exams) the zzz button just isn't there, so it feels half-finished.

On the reward half, I genuinely want the ceremony, not an inline card that quietly appears. The killer detail for me is honesty-of-pattern plus the things variant 1 lacks: the 7-dot week heatmap (Habits already gives me a flame+streak via the exact entries[todayKey]=true / backward-walk pattern this reuses, and it stung that clearing my day gave me nothing comparable), AND the manual "Close out the day" button. Real student days are never a clean zero, my list is messy, so an auto-card that only fires on a perfect empty day would basically never trigger for me; the manual close-out lets me say "I'm done, roll the rest" even with 2 things left. And the "Roll M unfinished -> tomorrow" seeding dailyPlan[tomorrowKey] via onDailyPlanChange means tomorrow morning opens already planned, which is the actual payoff of the loop, not just a streak number. onDailyPlanChange exists in ctx, so this is real, not hand-wavy.

I'd pass on Variant 3 (Triage Mode) even though it's the flashiest. A full focused one-card-at-a-time overlay with a J/K state machine is a power-user/inbox-zero ritual; for me it's friction and a big surface I'd open once out of novelty then ignore, and it's the biggest build for the most risk. I want snooze to be an invisible per-row reflex everywhere plus a satisfying end-of-day moment, which is exactly variant 2. It also respects the constraint cleanly: imported calendar rows have negative, non-topic-owned ids, so they correctly get no snooze, and the hook centralizes that one audited mutation path. votes.preferred: Shared useSnooze hook + dedicated EndOfDay close-out ritual
votes.why:
As Tom, a freelancer, my list does not naturally hit zero like a student's might. My priorities are a churning mix: client deadlines, follow-up emails I dated, invoicing tasks. Two things about this variant fit my actual day better than the others.

First, the snooze half being a shared primitive matters because I do not live in one widget. I bounce between TodayWidget on the home grid, the DeadlinesView when I'm planning a week, and I scan UpcomingDeadlinesWidget for what's bearing down. Variant 1 only wires snooze into TodayWidget + DeadlinesView and treats Upcoming as out of scope; that means "push to tomorrow" behaves inconsistently depending on which surface I happen to be looking at, which is exactly the kind of papercut that makes me stop trusting a tool. The hook (useSnooze(ctx) calling the single onTopicChange path I verified in context.ts) makes the gesture identical everywhere, including the free Upcoming bonus, and the detail that snooze only re-dates deadline/dueMin means a "bumped to tomorrow" state is detectable and self-labeling. That coherence is the whole point of an action layer.

Second, the close-out being a manual, always-present "Close out the day" button in the footer, not just an auto-card that appears at zero, is the decisive factor for me. I almost never end a day at literally zero actionable items, so Variant 1's ClearCard (which only swaps in when today+overdue hit zero) would essentially never fire for me, and the reward/streak machinery would be dead weight. Variant 3's Triage Mode has the same problem amplified: it only resolves into celebration when the LAST card clears, so the finish-line and streak are gated on a clean sweep I rarely achieve. A freelancer's "done for the day" is a decision I make at 6pm with three things still open, not a state the app detects. The manual fire lets me claim the close-out and roll the rest forward, which is how I actually work.

The roll-forward in this variant is also the strongest version: it re-dates owned items AND seeds dailyPlan[tomorrowKey] via onDailyPlanChange (both real ctx paths I confirmed), so tomorrow opens pre-planned instead of me re-triaging from scratch. Variants 1 and 3 only re-date; they leave tomorrow as an undifferentiated pile.

On reward, the 7-dot week heatmap is honestly the thing that would keep me coming back. I respond to visible consistency more than to a one-shot checkmark, and it directly closes the "habits gets a heatmap, this gets nothing" gap. The clearLog implementation reusing HabitsWidget's exact entries[todayKey]=true + backward-walk currentStreak pattern (which I read) means it's a low-risk, proven mechanism, not new infrastructure.

Why not Variant 3 despite it being the boldest: a full keyboard state machine and focused single-card overlay is a lot of build for a flow that assumes I want to process my whole list to empty in one sitting. As a freelancer I dip in and out; a modal "process my morning" ritual fights my interruption-driven day, and the celebration being locked behind clearing every card means I'd rarely see it. Variant 2 gives me the same one-keystroke 1/2/3 snooze on the row (the literal promise) without forcing me into a mode, plus a reward I can actually trigger. Variant 1 is the safe pick but it under-delivers on both halves for a non-student: snooze isn't app-wide, and the celebration almost never triggers for my workload. Variant 2's cost (one component + one hook) is modest and buys consistency plus a reward that fits real adult/freelancer behavior, which is why I'd actually use it.

votes.preferred: Shared useSnooze hook + dedicated EndOfDay close-out ritual
votes.why:
As a power user I push deadlines constantly, so the question that decides this for me is "is snooze the same everywhere?" — and only this variant guarantees that. I verified in the code that snooze has exactly one safe place to live: onTopicChange is the single audited mutation path (Dashboard.tsx:1872 maps next straight into setTopics), and priorities() in TodayWidget recomputes purely from ctx.topics, so a re-date re-buckets a row instantly. Extracting that into useSnooze(ctx) and dropping <SnoozeMenu> with 1/2/3 hotkeys into PrioritiesSection rows, DeadlinesView rows AND UpcomingDeadlinesWidget rows means "bump to tomorrow" behaves identically on every surface I touch. That consistency is the whole point of "one-keystroke snooze on EVERY row" — variant 1 only wires the row snooze into Today (plus a reused helper on DeadlinesView), which leaves the Next-7-Days widget — the surface I actually scan most — as a dead list I can't act on. For a power user that asymmetry is exactly the friction this goal is meant to kill.

On the close-out half: I want the reward to FIRE ON DEMAND, not only when an automatic counter happens to hit zero. Variant 1's auto-celebrating card only appears when actionable items transition to zero with >=1 earlier; in real student life I close out my day with two things still half-dated, so I'd rarely see it. The always-present "Close out the day" button in TodayWidget's footer lets me trigger the ritual whenever I decide the day is done — that matches how I actually work. It also pays the two debts the brief names: the 7-dot week heatmap directly answers "Habits gets a heatmap, this gets nothing," and clearLog reuses the proven HabitsWidget save/reload + backward-walk streak code I read (entries[todayKey]=true), so the streak is trustworthy rather than a new bespoke path. The killer detail is "Roll M unfinished -> tomorrow" seeding dailyPlan[tomorrowKey] via onDailyPlanChange (which exists in ctx) — that's not just re-dating, it means tomorrow opens pre-planned, closing the loop into the next morning's brief. That forward-link is what makes this an actual daily ritual instead of a one-off animation.

I rejected variant 3 (Triage Mode) despite it being the boldest: a focused one-card-at-a-time overlay with a J/K/1-4 state machine is great the first week, but as a power user with a long list I want to snooze three specific rows inline in two seconds and move on — not be forced through a serialized inbox flow that hides the list I'm trying to scan. It also concentrates all the risk in a new keyboard state machine for the LEAST reusable payoff. Variant 1 is the safe ship but under-delivers on "every row" and gates the reward behind an empty-list condition I rarely hit. Variant 2 is the right altitude: one hook + one component buys me consistent snooze across all three deadline surfaces AND an on-demand, heatmap-backed, plan-seeding close-out — the most coherent, dogfood-worthy read of the goal, and the build cost (one hook + one component, all on existing audited paths) is honestly accounted for.

changes.title: Add effortMin to TopicItem + a shared effort heuristic/format lib (the keystone field)
changes.approach: Add optional `effortMin?: number | null` to TopicItem (lib/types.ts:21-35), mirroring the proven optional `dueMin` pattern exactly so it is migration-safe (undefined = unestimated, no migration needed). Create new src/lib/effort.ts as the single source of truth: (a) SIZE buckets S=15 / M=60 / L=180 min with labels and colours; (b) `estimateEffort(text): number` — the 'Auto-Estimated, User-Corrected' winner — a pure, offline keyword heuristic (essay/report/dissertation/revise/project => L; read/email/call/buy/print/submit/short => S; default M), reusing the same verb/noun vocabulary already in lib/taskParser.ts ACTION_VERB so it stays consistent; (c) `effortToSize`/`sizeToMin`/`effortLabel` helpers. Extend the DeadlineEntry projection in widgets/util.ts to carry `effortMin` (it already carries dueMin at line 64) so every consumer (sort, nudge, planner) reads one field. No write sites yet — pure additive types + lib. Build FIRST; everything else imports from here.
changes.risk: Very low. Purely additive optional field + new isolated lib file; TypeScript stays green because effortMin is optional everywhere and util.ts already spreads the item fields.

changes.title: Capture effort everywhere tasks are born: auto-estimate on create, S/M/L chips to correct
changes.approach: Wire the keystone field into every creation/triage path. (1) In Dashboard.tsx, set `effortMin: estimateEffort(text)` in the three TopicItem literals — addTopicItem (line 771), addDeadline newItem (line 800), and fileAllPredicted (line 840) — so EVERY captured task is auto-estimated the moment it lands (the 'Auto-Estimated' half of the winner). (2) Add a tiny shared `EffortChips` control (three S/M/L pills, current size highlighted) and render it: in BriefingView's BriefingDeadlineBar (next to the parsed-deadline chip, ~line 74) and DeadlineQuickAdd's expanded input — both currently call addDeadline; thread an optional effortMin through addDeadline's signature (default to estimate) so the user can override before commit. (3) In InboxView's InboxRow, show the auto-estimated size as an editable chip beside the DatePicker (line 119) and pass the chosen effortMin through onAssign -> addTopicItem (this is the 'User-Corrected' half at triage time). (4) In TopicTodosWidget, render EffortChips in the item row next to DeadlineControl (line 136) wired through the existing updateItem/onTopicChange path (line 301-302). One field, captured at birth, correctable at four surfaces.
changes.risk: Medium. Touches five files and widens addDeadline/onAssign signatures; keep params optional with estimate fallback so older call sites compile. UI-only chips; no data migration. Main risk is signature threading — contained by defaulting effortMin.

changes.title: Effort-aware sort + a red 'start now' warning banner on the Deadlines hub and the brief
changes.approach: Pay off the keystone immediately, independent of the planner. Add a pure `pressure(due, effortMin, freeMinBeforeDue): {level, label}` helper to lib/effort.ts that compares time-left against a conservative free-time-before-due estimate (waking hours minus today's events) and flags 'not enough time' when effort approaches/exceeds the gap. In DeadlinesView, within each bucket replace the pure `dueTimestamp` sort (line 94) with a secondary sort that floats high-pressure (big + soon) items to the top of their bucket, and render an effort dot/label (from effortLabel) on each row beside the urgency label (line 258). At the very top of DeadlinesView and inside TodayWidget's brief, render a single red banner — 'Start now: "Stats essay" is big (~3h) and there isn't enough free time before Fri' — for the single most at-risk item, reusing the alert theme tokens (t.alert/alertBg/alertBorder) already used by PrioritiesSection. This is the CTO's 'fire a red banner' deliverable and proves the field earns its keep before P2.
changes.risk: Low-medium. Sort + render changes over existing data; the free-time estimate is heuristic (a label, not a hard claim) so being approximate is acceptable. No persistence, no new state.

changes.title: Auto-Plan My Day: a derive-on-the-fly timed schedule on the Briefing (shared free-slot engine)
changes.approach: The signature DOING move, built on the 'Inline Brief Schedule (zero new persistence)' winner. Create src/lib/dayPlanner.ts with the ONE shared free-slot engine the agenda demands: `freeGaps(todayEvents, now, dayBounds)` inverts today's timed events (using getStartMin/getEndMin logic already in TodayWidget.tsx:44-54) into open intervals; `autoPlan(priorities, gaps, effortMin)` greedily lays unscheduled due/overdue/today tasks into gaps as in-memory timed blocks, returning {scheduled:[{task,startMin,endMin}], bumped:[task]}. This is the same gap math feeding the P3 'start now' warning — built once. In TodayWidget, add an 'Auto-plan my day' button at the top of the Priorities section that toggles an inline render: 'Free 10:00-12:00 -> Stats essay · 14:00-15:00 -> reading' plus an honest 'your day is full — 2 rolled to tomorrow' line. Derived on the fly from priorities()+todayEvents+effortMin; NOTHING persisted (no PlannerItem/dailyPlan write), exactly the chosen variant. Surfaced in BriefingView since it forces showTasks on. Converts 'morning in 90 seconds' (glance) into 'day planned in 90 seconds' (plan).
changes.risk: Medium. New engine + new UI block, but read-only/derived so it cannot corrupt state. Depends on effortMin (change 1/2) for realistic block lengths; falls back to size M when unestimated. Keep the block list capped/scrollable to fit the widget.

changes.title: Shared useSnooze hook: one-keystroke defer on every priority + deadline row
changes.approach: The 'Shared useSnooze hook' winner — the triage half of the retention loop. Create src/lib/useSnooze.ts exposing `snooze(entry, to: 'tomorrow'|'weekend'|'nextWeek')` that rewrites the item's deadline (and preserves dueMin/effortMin) via the existing onTopicChange path in ctx (context.ts:59), computing the new local-midnight target. Add a compact snooze affordance (a small clock/'->' control opening tomorrow/weekend/next-week) to each row in TodayWidget's PrioritiesSection (line 195-227) and each DeadlinesView row (line 236). Because calendar-imported entries have negative synthetic ids (util.eventItemId) and no topic, the hook no-ops/ hides snooze for those (topic items only) — verified those rows are distinguishable by section being a real topic id. This is the single most common morning action and is the connective tissue that makes the planner's 'bumped to tomorrow' and the close-out's 'roll unfinished' feel like one system.
changes.risk: Medium. Mutates real topic deadlines via onTopicChange (well-trodden path). Must guard against snoozing synthetic calendar entries (no topic to write to) — handled by checking the entry resolves to a real topic item before showing the control.

changes.title: End-of-Day close-out ritual with a persisted clear-streak + roll-unfinished-to-tomorrow
changes.approach: The reward half of the loop — the 'dedicated EndOfDay close-out ritual' winner. When today's actionable priorities (overdue+today, computed at TodayWidget.tsx:498-499) reach zero, render a new EndOfDayCard: a celebratory 'Today's done — N cleared' card with a daily clear-streak. Persist the streak in the SAME local+sync pattern Habits uses: add a `clearStreak?: { entries: Record<string,true>; }`-shaped store (a new `clearLog` state in Dashboard mirroring the `dailyPlan` save effect at Dashboard.tsx:479 and the keys array at line 173-180), reusing the exact currentStreak walk-back from HabitsWidget. Add a one-tap 'Roll unfinished to tomorrow' that, for any still-open today/overdue topic items, calls the same deadline-rewrite used by useSnooze (set deadline = tomorrow), seeding tomorrow's brief instead of letting items rot into overdue shame. Card composes over data TodayWidget already has; the streak is the only new persisted state and follows the proven habits sync shape.
changes.risk: Medium. Adds one new persisted key (clearLog) — follows the exact dailyPlan/habits save+load+sync pattern, so within the proven local+Supabase blob (no schema/auth touched). Roll-to-tomorrow reuses the snooze rewrite, so no new mutation path. Keep streak state optional/back-compat (absent = streak 0).

plan.summary: Six grounded, sequenced changes that deliver this round's three agenda priorities using the user-chosen experiment variants. The spine is the keystone effort field: change 1 adds optional `effortMin` to TopicItem (mirroring the proven optional `dueMin` pattern in lib/types.ts) plus a single isolated lib/effort.ts holding the offline auto-estimate heuristic and S/M/L buckets — built first because everything downstream consumes it. Change 2 captures it everywhere tasks are born (auto-estimated in Dashboard's three TopicItem write sites, user-correctable via S/M/L chips in the briefing bar, deadline quick-add, Quicks triage, and the in-topic row) — the 'Auto-Estimated, User-Corrected' winner. Change 3 cashes the field in immediately with effort-aware sorting and a red 'start now' warning on the Deadlines hub and brief, independent of the planner. Change 4 ships the signature 'Auto-Plan My Day' as a derive-on-the-fly inline schedule with zero new persistence (the chosen variant), built on a single freeGaps engine shared with change 3's warning. Changes 5 and 6 are the two halves of the retention loop per the winning variant: a shared useSnooze hook deferring any row via the existing onTopicChange path, and a dedicated EndOfDay close-out card with a clear-streak persisted in the exact habits/dailyPlan sync pattern plus a roll-unfinished-to-tomorrow that reuses the snooze rewrite. Every change is self-contained React/TS over existing WidgetCtx state, keeps TypeScript green via optional fields, and touches no auth/payments/secrets/schema/CI — the only new persisted state (clearLog) follows the proven local+Supabase blob pattern. Verified against real files: TopicItem at lib/types.ts:21-35 (no effort field), the dueMin threading through DeadlineControl/util.dueTimestamp, the addTopicItem/addDeadline/fileAllPredicted write sites in Dashboard.tsx:762-852, priorities() date-only sort at TodayWidget.tsx:70-86, dormant PlannerItem at types.ts:216, and the habits streak walk-back template in HabitsWidget.tsx.
